summary: Stancil revisits his old “metrics layer” idea, arguing it stalled less for technical reasons than for economic ones: horizontal layers are hard to sell without owning the application. He suggests AI-driven “context layers” face the same problem, even if they’re increasingly necessary as agents need governed, shared context across tools. The post ends by weighing two futures—agents embedded in apps versus standalone specialist agents—and doubting whether the market will support the cleaner separation.
money_quotes: Though centralized horizontal layers sound nice, you have to have something to sell.
And what’s best for the customer—a tight architecture of mutually exclusive and collectively exhaustive parts—may not be what’s best for the businesses making the stuff.
You can’t onboard an analyst by giving them logins to a bunch of tools; you can’t make a good bot by granting it access to the same sources.
The road to hell is paved with practicalities.
It’s not a SaaS app with a specialized bot inside; it’s a specialized bot that can use SaaS apps.
key_insight: The context/metrics layer is conceptually elegant but economically fragile, and AI agents are likely to recreate the same incentives that keep context bundled inside applications.
